Core Mechanism & Structure: The model employs a multi-scale approach to time series forecasting. It first normalizes inputs using a RevIN-like affine transformation. Then, for multiple temporal scales, it projects input sequences into a continuous-time representation using HiPPO (High-order Polynomial Projection Operator) with Legendre polynomials, obtaining a sequence of HiPPO coefficients. These coefficients are then processed by a 1D Spectral Convolution layer in the frequency domain to mix features. Finally, the processed HiPPO coefficients are reconstructed back to the time domain, and outputs from different scales are aggregated via a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) and de-normalized.
Core Innovations: Integration of HiPPO Legendre projections with 1D Spectral Convolutions: The model combines continuous-time signal modeling via HiPPO with frequency-domain mixing using spectral convolutions, applying the latter directly to the HiPPO coefficients rather than raw time series.

Multi-scale processing architecture: The model processes input data at multiple temporal granularities (defined by `multiscale` factors), each handled by a dedicated HiPPO and Spectral Convolution branch, allowing for capture of diverse temporal patterns.

Implicit handling of non-stationarity: It incorporates an instance-wise normalization and de-normalization scheme with learnable affine parameters, similar to RevIN, to robustly handle shifts in data distribution.

Key Technical Details.Input Embedding: The raw input time series `x_enc` undergoes an initial normalization (subtracting mean, dividing by standard deviation) followed by learnable affine transformations (`affine_weight`, `affine_bias`). The `HiPPO_LegT` then projects segments of this input into continuous Legendre polynomial coefficients, serving as a form of continuous-time representation rather than a discrete embedding.
Key Technical Details.Feature Extraction: Feature extraction is performed in two main stages: 1) `HiPPO_LegT` transforms the time series into a sequence of state vectors (Legendre coefficients) capturing continuous dynamics. 2) `SpectralConv1d` applies 1D Fourier transforms, performs linear mixing in the frequency domain (only considering a limited number of low-frequency `modes`), and then inverse Fourier transforms the HiPPO coefficients. This efficiently mixes information within the continuous representation.
Key Technical Details.Positional Encoding: No explicit positional encoding module is used. Temporal information is implicitly handled by the continuous-time dynamics of the `HiPPO_LegT` state-space model, which maintains a state representation that evolves over time.
Key Technical Details.Non-stationarity Handling: The model explicitly handles non-stationarity through a normalization and de-normalization procedure applied per instance (mean subtraction, standard deviation division) before and after the core model computations. It also includes learnable `affine_weight` and `affine_bias` parameters, a technique broadly known as Reversible Instance Normalization (RevIN).

Direct Observations.evidence: Class `HiPPO_LegT` is defined in `models\FiLM.py` and instantiated as `self.legts = nn.ModuleList([HiPPO_LegT(...)])` within `Model.__init__`.

Class `SpectralConv1d` is defined in `models\FiLM.py` and instantiated as `self.spec_conv_1 = nn.ModuleList([SpectralConv1d(...)])` within `Model.__init__`. Its `forward` method uses `torch.fft.rfft` and `torch.fft.irfft`.

`self.multiscale = [1, 2, 4]` is defined in `Model.__init__`, and `self.legts` and `self.spec_conv_1` are `nn.ModuleList`s where instances are created for each scale (`for n in self.window_size for i in self.multiscale`). The input length `x_in_len = self.multiscale[i % len(self.multiscale)] * self.pred_len` adapts per branch.

Direct Observations.claim: Handles non-stationarity with instance-wise normalization/de-normalization and affine transforms (RevIN-like)
Direct Observations.evidence: In `Model.forecast` (and other task methods), the lines `means = x_enc.mean(1, keepdim=True).detach()`, `x_enc = x_enc - means`, `stdev = torch.sqrt(torch.var(x_enc, dim=1, keepdim=True, unbiased=False) + 1e-5).detach()`, `x_enc /= stdev`, `x_enc = x_enc * self.affine_weight + self.affine_bias` for normalization, and similar reversed steps for de-normalization, are explicitly present.

Direct Observations.claim: Supports multiple task types including forecasting, imputation, anomaly detection, and classification
Direct Observations.evidence: The `Model.forward` method contains conditional branches based on `self.task_name` (`'long_term_forecast'`, `'short_term_forecast'`, `'imputation'`, `'anomaly_detection'`, `'classification'`), each calling a dedicated method.

Direct Observations.claim: Applies spectral convolution to HiPPO coefficients
Direct Observations.evidence: In `Model.forecast`, `x_in_c = legt(x_in.transpose(1, 2)).permute([1, 2, 3, 0])` first gets HiPPO coefficients, and then `out1 = self.spec_conv_1[i](x_in_c)` applies spectral convolution to these coefficients.

Direct Observations.claim: Paper Link is provided in code comments

Strengths.label: Long Sequence Modeling
Strengths.why: HiPPO is designed to maintain high-fidelity representations of long sequences with continuous updates, while Spectral Convolutions operate efficiently in the frequency domain, capturing global patterns without the quadratic complexity of full attention mechanisms.
Strengths.conditions: Especially effective when sequence lengths are very long (thousands or tens of thousands of steps) where attention becomes prohibitive.

Strengths.label: Robustness to Non-stationarity
Strengths.why: The explicit per-instance normalization and de-normalization with learnable affine parameters helps the model adapt to shifts in mean and variance across different time series instances or time periods.
Strengths.conditions: When the underlying data distribution changes over time or across different batches/series.

Strengths.label: Multi-scale Pattern Capture
Strengths.why: The use of multiple `multiscale` factors with independent HiPPO and Spectral Convolution branches allows the model to simultaneously analyze and integrate patterns from different temporal horizons or granularities.
Strengths.conditions: When time series exhibit important patterns at various frequencies or temporal scales.

Strengths.label: Computational Efficiency (vs. Attention)
Strengths.why: Replacing attention with HiPPO (O(L*N^2) or O(L*N) if N is small) and Spectral Convolutions (O(L log L) + O(F*N^2)) can lead to better scalability for very long sequences compared to O(L^2) attention.
Strengths.conditions: When the sequence length 'L' is much larger than the HiPPO order 'N', and the number of frequency 'modes' is small.

Weaknesses.label: High Computational Cost for Large HiPPO Order (N)
Weaknesses.why: The `HiPPO_LegT` update and `SpectralConv1d` einsum operations involve `N^2` complexity (where N=`window_size`=256), leading to a significant computational burden per time step and per feature channel. For a typical time series problem, `N=256` results in `N^2` operations, which can be computationally intensive.
Weaknesses.conditions: When the HiPPO order (N) or the number of features (F) is large, or when processing very long sequences in a single batch.

Weaknesses.label: Reduced Interpretability
Weaknesses.why: HiPPO coefficients and frequency-domain transformations are less directly interpretable than attention weights in Transformer models, making it harder to understand which past events or features contribute most to a prediction.
Weaknesses.conditions: When model explainability is a primary requirement for decision-making.

Weaknesses.label: Hyperparameter Sensitivity
Weaknesses.why: The model introduces several critical hyperparameters (e.g., HiPPO order `N`, discretization step `dt`, number of `modes` in SpectralConv1d, `multiscale` factors) that require careful tuning for optimal performance across different datasets.
Weaknesses.conditions: When applying to diverse datasets without extensive hyperparameter search.

Weaknesses.label: Limited Expressiveness for Highly Irregular Patterns
Weaknesses.why: While efficient for continuous signals, the fixed Legendre basis of HiPPO and the reliance on low-frequency modes in SpectralConv1d might limit the model's ability to capture extremely complex, non-periodic, or highly irregular local temporal dependencies that attention mechanisms can dynamically learn.
Weaknesses.conditions: On highly noisy or extremely sparse time series data with non-smooth and non-linear dynamics.

Resource Requirements & Complexity:
The computational complexity of the model is dominated by the `HiPPO_LegT` and `SpectralConv1d` components for each of the `B_branches` (number of multi-scale branches). 
- For `HiPPO_LegT`, it's approximately `O(L_in * Batch * Features * N^2)` where `L_in` is the input sequence length for a branch, `N` is the HiPPO order (window_size=256), `Batch` is the batch size, and `Features` is the number of input features (`configs.enc_in`). This `N^2` term is significant due to `N=256`.
- For `SpectralConv1d`, it's approximately `O(Batch * Features * N * (L_in log L_in + modes * N))`, where `modes` is `min(32, L_in // 2)`. The `modes * N` term comes from the `einsum` in `compl_mul1d` due to the channel mixing dimension being `N` (HiPPO dim).
Overall, the model's complexity per branch is roughly `O(L_in * Batch * Features * N^2 + Batch * Features * N * L_in log L_in)`. The memory footprint includes storing the `A` and `B` matrices (`O(N^2)`) for each HiPPO module, and `weights_real`/`weights_imag` (`O(N^2 * modes)`) for each SpectralConv1d module, multiplied by the number of branches. Given `N=256`, the `N^2` factors imply high memory and computational demands for these core components.
Paper Abstract: Recent studies have shown that deep learning models such as RNNs and Transformers have brought significant performance gains for long-term forecasting of time series because they effectively utilize historical information. We found, however, that there is still great room for improvement in how to preserve historical information in neural networks while avoiding overfitting to noise presented in the history. Addressing this allows better utilization of the capabilities of deep learning models. To this end, we design a \textbf{F}requency \textbf{i}mproved \textbf{L}egendre \textbf{M}emory model, or {\bf FiLM}: it applies Legendre Polynomials projections to approximate historical information, uses Fourier projection to remove noise, and adds a low-rank approximation to speed up computation. Our empirical studies show that the proposed FiLM significantly improves the accuracy of state-of-the-art models in multivariate and univariate long-term forecasting by (\textbf{20.3\%}, \textbf{22.6\%}), respectively. We also demonstrate that the representation module developed in this work can be used as a general plug-in to improve the long-term prediction performance of other deep learning modules. Code is available at this https URL

Addressing this allows better utilization of the capabilities of deep learning models. To this end, we design a \textbf{F}requency \textbf{i}mproved \textbf{L}egendre \textbf{M}emory model, or {\bf FiLM}: it applies Legendre Polynomials projections to approximate historical information, uses Fourier projection to remove noise, and adds a low-rank approximation to speed up computation. paper_claims.claim: Deep learning models like RNNs and Transformers significantly improve long-term time series forecasting by effectively utilizing historical information.
There is still room for improvement in preserving historical information in neural networks while avoiding overfitting to noise.
The Frequency improved Legendre Memory (FiLM) model uses Legendre Polynomials projections to approximate historical information.
FiLM applies Fourier projection to remove noise from historical data.
FiLM incorporates a low-rank approximation to speed up computation.
FiLM improves state-of-the-art model accuracy by 20.3% for multivariate and 22.6% for univariate long-term forecasting.

论文摘要: Recent studies have shown that deep learning models such as RNNs and Transformers have brought significant performance gains for long-term forecasting of time series because they effectively utilize historical information. We found, however, that there is still great room for improvement in how to preserve historical information in neural networks while avoiding overfitting to noise presented in the history. Addressing this allows better utilization of the capabilities of deep learning models. To this end, we design a \textbf{F}requency \textbf{i}mproved \textbf{L}egendre \textbf{M}emory model, or {\bf FiLM}: it applies Legendre Polynomials projections to approximate historical information, uses Fourier projection to remove noise, and adds a low-rank approximation to speed up computation. Our empirical studies show that the proposed FiLM significantly improves the accuracy of state-of-the-art models in multivariate and univariate long-term forecasting by (\textbf{20.3\%}, \textbf{22.6\%}), respectively. We also demonstrate that the representation module developed in this work can be used as a general plug-in to improve the long-term prediction performance of other deep learning modules.
